full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Figuring out what to actually ablate.
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: i need to ablate 2 components of this offset-free svm. i was going to try changing C but my friend said thats not a component its a hyperparameter. what are actual algorithm components i can ablate?
full_llm_interaction_log.student_modification: N/A — component selection. I chose WSS and initialization after this conversation.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Listed genuine method components: Working Set Selection strategy (Section 4), initialization strategy (Section 2.2), stopping tolerance. I chose WSS and initialization as they are most clearly defined in the paper.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Re-read Section 4 and 2.2 to confirm these are explicit algorithmic design choices.

full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: for random WSS do i pick randomly from all n training points or only from the ones that are currently violating the KKT conditions
full_llm_interaction_log.student_modification: Implemented random selection over violating variables only. Implementation is entirely mine.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Clarified: only from violating variables is fairer — picking from non-violating ones wastes iterations doing nothing.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Confirmed against Section 4.1 of the paper.

full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: what would be a 'bad' starting point for alpha that is still feasible (0 <= alpha_i <= C). i want something that is clearly worse than starting at all zeros
full_llm_interaction_log.student_modification: I chose alpha = C/2 for all i. The reasoning for why this is bad is my own.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Suggested alpha = C/2 or alpha = C for all variables. I chose C/2 as it represents maximum uncertainty (midpoint of feasible range).

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding why accuracy is the same despite worse convergence.
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: both my ablations converged to same accuracy just slower. is that expected? i would have thought random WSS might find a different local minimum
full_llm_interaction_log.student_modification: N/A — conceptual check. Interpretation paragraph is fully my own writing.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Explained the dual is a concave quadratic so there is only one global maximum. Any valid coordinate ascent converges to the same solution — WSS only affects the path, not the destination.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Matches Theorem 1 guarantee in the paper.

top_5_prompts.prompt: i was going to ablate C but my friend said thats a hyperparameter not a component, what are actual algorithm components?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This completely changed my ablation plan. Ablating genuine paper-defined components (WSS, initialization) is much more credible than tuning C.

top_5_prompts.prompt: both ablations converged to same accuracy just slower — is that expected or did i do something wrong?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This is the key insight. The dual is concave = unique maximum = every valid strategy finds it. Core to the ablation interpretation.

top_5_prompts.prompt: for random WSS do i pick from all n points or only violating ones?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This implementation detail affects whether the comparison is fair.

top_5_prompts.prompt: what would be a 'bad' starting point for alpha that is still feasible?
top_5_prompts.why_important: Needed a principled choice for Ablation 2, not just an arbitrary starting point.
